skill.name: twitter-automation
skill.description: Automate Twitter/X posting, media publishing, engagement actions, direct messages, and profile lookups through the inference.sh CLI.
skill.summary: Run Twitter/X account workflows from an AI assistant using inference.sh commands.
skill.author: inference-shell

security_audit.summary: The static Ruby backtick and weak cryptography findings are false positives caused by Markdown code spans and the Twitter/X wording. The skill still carries medium risk because it grants an external CLI permission to perform networked, state-changing actions on authenticated Twitter/X accounts, including posts, DMs, follows, likes, retweets, and deletion.

medium_findings.title: External CLI Can Perform State-Changing Twitter/X Actions
medium_findings.description: The skill instructs the assistant to run inference.sh commands that post, like, retweet, send direct messages, follow users, retrieve data, and delete posts. This appears aligned with the skill purpose, but it can modify an authenticated social account and should require user confirmation before each action.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The commands and app names clearly describe account-changing Twitter/X operations. The intent is legitimate automation, so this is a controlled medium risk rather than confirmed malicious behavior.

medium_findings.title: Networked Media Workflows Can Publish External Content
medium_findings.description: The skill provides workflows that generate media through external apps and publish media URLs to Twitter/X. This can expose generated content, remote media URLs, and account activity through third-party services.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The workflow explicitly combines external generation services with public social posting. No credential exfiltration is shown, but the data-sharing and publication behavior is evident.

low_findings.title: Static Analyzer False Positives for Backticks and Weak Crypto
low_findings.description: The reported Ruby backtick execution findings are Markdown code spans and fenced shell examples, not Ruby source execution. The weak cryptography findings appear to be triggered by ordinary Twitter/X text and table content, not cryptographic code.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited file is Markdown documentation, and the flagged text is either inline code formatting or command examples. No Ruby code, cryptographic API usage, or hash algorithm implementation is present.

dangerous_patterns.title: Broad External Command Authorization
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill permits Bash execution for inference.sh commands. This is necessary for the skill, but it delegates account actions and network calls to an external CLI.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The allowed tool declaration and quick-start command directly authorize and demonstrate CLI execution. The risk is bounded to the infsh command pattern.

dangerous_patterns.title: Authenticated Social Network Operations
dangerous_patterns.description: Several commands act on Twitter/X resources through networked app calls. These operations can affect public account state or private conversations.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The listed examples include likes, retweets, DMs, follows, and deletion using Twitter/X app identifiers. These are clearly authenticated social account actions.

content.value_statement: Managing Twitter/X actions by hand can slow content teams and technical operators. This skill uses inference.sh commands to post, engage, message, and retrieve account data from Claude, Codex, or Claude Code.
actual_capabilities: Posts text updates to Twitter/X through the x/post-tweet app.
Creates posts with media URLs through the x/post-create app.
Likes, retweets, follows users, and deletes posts by ID.
Sends direct messages when a recipient ID and message are provided.
Retrieves user profiles and post details through inference.sh apps.
Shows workflows for publishing generated images or videos.
limitations: Requires a configured inference.sh CLI and authenticated Twitter/X integration.
Does not provide native scheduling logic inside the skill file.
Depends on external service availability and Twitter/X API permissions.
Can publish or modify account content, so user confirmation is important.

use_cases.title: Publish Campaign Updates
use_cases.description: Draft and post approved campaign messages with optional media from an assistant workflow.

use_cases.title: Operate Developer Announcements
use_cases.description: Share release notes, retrieve post details, and manage engagement actions from a terminal-based assistant.

use_cases.title: Coordinate Support Outreach
use_cases.description: Look up user profiles and send direct messages after a human approves each interaction.

prompt_templates.title: Post a Simple Update
prompt_templates.prompt: Post this approved update to Twitter/X: [message]. Show me the exact action before running it.

prompt_templates.title: Create a Media Post
prompt_templates.scenario: Publishing a post with a media URL
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a Twitter/X post using this text and media URL. Confirm the text, media source, and account action before posting.

prompt_templates.title: Review Engagement Actions
prompt_templates.prompt: Prepare an engagement plan for these post IDs and usernames. Ask for approval before any like, retweet, follow, or delete action.

prompt_templates.title: Run a Generated Media Workflow
prompt_templates.prompt: Generate media from this concept, prepare a Twitter/X post, and summarize every external service and public output before execution.

output_examples.input: Post an approved product launch message.
output_examples.output: The assistant confirms the final text, identifies the Twitter/X posting action, runs the inference.sh app after approval, and reports the result.

output_examples.input: Share an image from a provided media URL.
output_examples.output: The assistant prepares a media post, checks the URL and caption with the user, publishes it through inference.sh, and summarizes the post status.

output_examples.input: Send a direct message to a known recipient ID.
output_examples.output: The assistant repeats the recipient and message for confirmation, sends the DM only after approval, and reports whether the command succeeded.

best_practices: Confirm every public post, direct message, follow, like, retweet, and deletion before execution.
Use least-privilege Twitter/X credentials and rotate them according to your organization policy.
Review generated media and remote URLs before publishing them to a social account.
anti_patterns: Do not run bulk engagement actions without rate limits, review, and platform compliance checks.
Do not place secrets, access tokens, or private user data in prompt text or post content.
Do not allow autonomous posting from untrusted prompts or unreviewed generated content.

faq.question: Does this skill require inference.sh?
faq.answer: Yes. The documented workflows depend on the inference.sh CLI and its configured apps.

faq.question: Can it post media to Twitter/X?
faq.answer: Yes. The skill shows media posting through a media URL using the x/post-create app.

faq.question: Can it schedule tweets by itself?
faq.answer: No. The skill description mentions scheduling, but the file does not define a scheduling workflow.

faq.question: Is it safe for autonomous social posting?
faq.answer: No. Human approval is recommended before any account-changing action.

faq.question: What tools can use this skill?
faq.answer: The report lists Claude, Codex, and Claude Code as supported tools.

faq.question: Why is the risk level medium?
faq.answer: The skill is not malicious, but it can run external network commands that change a Twitter/X account.
